items.title: US-China priority is 'economic relationship':  Trump 'heavily prioritising trade and investment'
items.description: Nadia Massih is pleased to welcome David Sacks, Fellow for Asia Studies at the Council on Foreign Relations (CFR). Questions surrounding Taiwan, semiconductor sovereignty, trade diplomacy, and Middle Eastern energy security are becoming inseparable components of a broader strategic contest between the United States and China. Sachs maps a relationship defined not by dramatic confrontation but by selective silence, transactional prioritization, and carefully managed ambiguity. Whether observing that “Taiwan doesn’t really figure on the agenda” for Washington, warning that allowing Chinese access to advanced semiconductors “would be a bad thing,” or noting that Beijing “is not going to just act as a proxy for US interests,” Sachs highlights the hidden mechanics of great power competition.

items.description: Donald Trump is the first US leader to visit Beijing in nearly a decade. Amid the pomp, circumstance and high-level meetings, Angela Diffley welcomes Shanhui ZHANG, Journalist at CGTN (China Global Television Network) and Fellow at the Center for China and Globalisation. From the outset, Zhang frames the relationship in almost symbolic and ritual terms, suggesting that “when the two top leaders are talking together, that’s how we can decide the future of the China and the U.S. relations.” The discussion unfolds around two distinct leadership dynamics and how they intersect with contested geopolitical issues such as Taiwan, Ukraine and the Middle East. Beijing sees itself as a world-class mediator and “responsible power that looks like what a power should be in the 21st century.”
